title: Module 1
introduction: This module is about lifting your tongue smoothly and evenly.
objectives: Perform click and tick-tock exercises to strengthen tongue and mid face muscles
Complete tongue brushing for neuromuscular re-education
Practice tongue trace movements to stretch the frenum
Establish baseline breathing health with BOLT test
Maintain tongue-to-spot positioning with elastic hold exercise

exercises.name: Clicks and Tick-Tocks
exercises.description:
Clicks (1 minute): Make repeated clicking sounds with tongue while keeping cheeks back. Have your tongue suctioned to the roof of your mouth for 3 seconds before clicking. Keep your lips back in a smile.

Tick-Tocks (1 minute): Make clicking sounds as fast as you can with good technique. This time moving lips from an exaggerated smile and do one click sound to an exaggerated pursed lip and do one click.
compensations: Watch in the mirror to prevent neck engagement, jaw protrusion and jaw lateralization. Use bite block to avoid

exercises.name: Brushing
exercises.description: Stick out the tongue and brush the full tongue 5 times. Brush the spot area 5 times. Place two surfaces together. Repeat for 1 minute. This exercise promotes neuromuscular re-education through sensory input
compensations: Watch in mirror to avoid facial grimace (rounding the lips or dimpling the chin) when protruding tongue
Continue nasal breathing throughout exercise (Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first)

exercises.name: Tongue Trace
exercises.description: Try with your mouth open, halfway or closed. Place the tip of your tongue against the spot and trace back along the palate in a straight line and far back as possible. You should feel a stretch in the frenum and floor of your mouth.
compensations: Watch in the mirror for jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion. Use bite block to avoid
Scan for head/neck/facial/body tension and try to ease

exercises.name: BOLT Test
exercises.description:
To be done on day 1, only needs to be done once.

This test measures breathing health and progress. Use the method of breathing you would have used prior to learning about myofunctional therapy.

Scale of what is considered healthy (higher score means organs/tissues are receiving adequate oxygen):
• 40-60 seconds is healthy and ideal
• 30-39 seconds indicates mild breathing/airway issues
• 20-29 seconds indicates moderate breathing/airway issues
• 10-19 seconds indicates unhealthy breathing
• 0-9 seconds is very unhealthy breathing and severe airway issues

Instructions:
1. Take a small, silent breath in and a small, silent breath out.
2. Hold your nose with your fingers to prevent air from entering your lungs while timing yourself 
3. At the first sign of "air hunger", you will also feel the first involuntary movements of your breathing muscles. Your stomach may jerk, or the area around your neck may contract.
4. Release your nose and stop the timer at these first signs of "air hunger". This will tell you your initial score.

Tips:
- When you release your nose, your inhalation should remain calm. If you are breathing heavily or taking deep breaths, then you waited too long and the score is not correct. Keep in mind that this is not a breath-holding test.

exercises.name: Tongue Tip – One Elastic Hold
exercises.description:
Place elastic on the tip of the tongue and hold it to the spot

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

checklist.label: BOLT Test completed and score documented

title: Module 2
introduction: This module, we retrain your swallow.
objectives: Strengthen posterior tongue muscles by practicing lingual palatal suction
Improve tongue coordination and tongue-mandible dissociation through tongue point exercise
Develop facial muscle tone and improve lip seal with cheek puffs
Practice diaphragmatic breathing
Maintain proper mid-tongue to palate positioning with elastic hold exercise

exercises.name: Lingual Palatal Suction
exercises.description:
Keep your lips back in a smile. Open mouth as wide as possible without floor of mouth lifting. Use strong suction to hold the tongue to the roof of mouth and hold for as long as possible (Start with 10 seconds and work up to 1 minute). Try focusing on lifting the back portion of the tongue to the palate. If tongue is overflowing you can slowly open and close teeth together to help tongue squish inside the arch. Use provided chopsticks to push tongue inside of arch can also help.

This exercise is most important to master prior to frenectomy
compensations: Watch in mirror for facial grimace (chin strain/dimpling), neck engagement, jaw protrusion and jaw lateralization, floor of mouth activation -- use bite block to avoid
Watch for tongue overflow over teeth. This is an important metric for tongue tie surgery

exercises.name: Tongue Points (Left and Right)
exercises.description: With a neutral face open half way. Using the smallest point of the tip of the tongue, touch the smallest surface at the corner of the mouth. Repeat for 2 minutes. Keep your lips back in a smile and open half way. Do the same tongue movements for another minute. Tongue movements should be slow and controlled without dragging the tongue along the lips.
compensations: Watch in mirror for jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion -- use bite block to avoid
Continue with nasal breathing (Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first)

exercises.name: Cheek Puffs
exercises.description: Fill your mouth with air. Move air into the right cheek and hold for 5 seconds. Move air into the left cheek and hold for 5 seconds. Move air into the top lip and hold for 5 seconds. Move air into the lower lip and hold for 5 seconds. Repeat for 2 minutes
compensations: Watch in the mirror for jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion. Keep teeth gently together to help avoid.
Continue with nasal breathing (Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first)

exercises.name: Belly/Diaphragm Breathing
exercises.description:
1. Lie on the floor face up with your knees slightly bent.
2. Place one hand lightly on your stomach and the other hand on your chest.
3. As you breathe, feel which hand is moving more, then begin to concentrate on breathing using your diaphragm (your belly), and not using your chest. Feel your stomach rise as your lungs fill with air from the bottom.
4. Let your stomach fall naturally when breathing out by relaxing your diaphragm.
5. Try placing a small object on your stomach, such as a small book or your cell phone, so that you can see the movement more easily and keep your breathing focused on that area.
6. Next, try this exercise standing or sitting up. Place your hands in the same position and focus your breathing the same way so that your stomach moves rather than your chest. This may take a bit more concentration.
7. Practice for 5 minutes at a time. Ideally, it is good to do this exercise first thing in the morning or at night before falling asleep because you are already in bed.

- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first

exercises.name: Middle of Tongue – One Elastic Hold
exercises.description:
Place elastic on the middle of the tongue and hold it to the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

title: Module 3
introduction: This module builds lateral tongue strength and establishes your breathing rhythm.
objectives: Strengthen lateral tongue muscles, improve coordination of the tongue and encourage tongue-mandible dissociation through tongue in cheek exercise
Improve posterior tongue elevation with K sounds
Improve tongue coordination, lip seal strength and tongue-mandible dissociation with tongue trace
Establish the 4-7-8 breathing pattern
Maintain proper mid-tongue to palate positioning with elastic hold exercise for an extended time

exercises.name: Tongue in Cheek
exercises.description: Push the tongue into the cheek without the jaw shifting and hold for 5 seconds. Practice with the mouth open and with lips closed together. Use slow and controlled movements. Do exercise with a still/relaxed face.
compensations: Watch in the mirror for jaw lateralization -- use bite block to avoid.
When the mouth is open, try to control for facial grimace (rounding of lips).
Check and prevent muscle bracing.

exercises.name: K Sounds (Kuh, Guh, Unk)
exercises.description: Make a clear, precise "kuh" sound with the mouth open (use a bite block and move to a taller position as you improve with exercise). The back of the tongue needs to raise up to make contact with the soft palate. The "kuh" sound helps facilitate this movement. Start with "kuh" and "guh" sounds the first week and switch to "guh" and "unk" sounds the second week of practice.
compensations: Watch in the mirror for jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion -- use bite block to avoid.
Control facial grimace (lips rounding)
Control neck engagement

exercises.name: Lip Trace
exercises.description:
While maintaining contact with the tongue under the lips, trace circles around the inside of the upper and lower lips. Alternate three times each direction. Keep your lips closed during this exercise.

Tracing with larger circles extending into the cheeks can add an extra stretch/challenge. Do smaller circles for patients who have more tongue restriction.
compensations: Watch in mirror to ensure as little jaw movement as possible - you may hold jaw or chin to minimize at first but try to limit over time
Minimize facial tension

exercises.name: 4-7-8 Breathing Pattern
exercises.description:
Instructions:
1. Set a timer for 5 minutes and begin.
2. Inhale counting to 4.
3. Before exhaling, hold your breath for a count of 7.
4. Exhale counting to 8.
5. Repeat for 5 minutes.

Tips:
- Do this anytime of day to help you relax or do it lying in bed to help you fall asleep.
- Remember to breathe only through your nose.
- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first
- Focus on inhaling the air into your belly/diaphragm.
- When you exhale, you may feel like you have to push out all your air. This is normal and helps promote deep breathing.

exercises.name: Middle of Tongue – One Elastic Hold
exercises.description:
Place elastic on the middle of the tongue and hold it to the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

title: Module 4 (Pre-Frenectomy)
introduction: This module prepares your tongue for the frenectomy. Master the suction hold and stretches before your procedure.
objectives: Improve tongue coordination, lip seal strength and tongue-mandible dissociation with tongue trace
Master the lingual palatal suction hold for 60 seconds (most important pre-frenectomy exercise)
Develop awareness of tongue overflow patterns
Learn manual stretching and release techniques
Recognize and reduce over-breathing habits
Maintain proper mid-tongue to palate positioning with elastic hold exercise for an extended time

exercises.name: Tongue Trace
exercises.description: Try with your mouth open, halfway or closed. Place the tip of tongue against the spot and trace back along the palate in a straight line and far back as possible. You should feel a stretch in the frenum and floor of your mouth.
compensations: Watch in mirror for jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion - use bite block to avoid
Scan for head/neck/facial/body tension and try to ease

exercises.name: Lingual Palatal Suction
exercises.description:
Keep your lips back in a smile. Open mouth as wide as possible without floor of mouth lifting. Use strong suction to hold the tongue to the roof of your mouth and hold for as long as possible (Start with 10 seconds and work up to 1 minute). Try focusing on lifting the back portion of the tongue to the palate. If tongue is overflowing you can slowly open and close teeth together to help tongue squish inside the arch. Use provided chopsticks to push tongue inside of arch can also help.

This exercise is most important to master prior to frenectomy
compensations: Watch in mirror for facial grimace (chin strain/dimpling), neck engagement, jaw protrusion and jaw lateralization, floor of mouth activation -- use bite block to avoid
Watch for tongue overflow over teeth. This is an important metric for tongue tie surgery

exercises.name: Tongue Stretch
exercises.description: Pull the tongue straight out of the mouth. Avoid tensing tongue, jaw, neck and face.

exercises.name: Forklift Stretch
exercises.description: Keeping tongue, jaw, neck and face relaxed, slide thumbs with pads facing upward underneath the tongue on either side of the frenum. Stretch the body of the tongue upward away from the floor of mouth

exercises.name: Floor of Mouth Release
exercises.description: Massage muscles along the floor of the mouth to release tension. Be slow and gentle, focusing on 1 area at a time.

exercises.name: Over Breathing Awareness
exercises.description:
When you stop mouth breathing and learn to bring your breathing volume toward normal, you have better oxygenation of your tissues and organs, including your brain.

Typical characteristics of over-breathing include mouth breathing, upper chest breathing, sighing, noticeable breathing during rest, and taking large breaths before talking

Instructions:
1. Start to become aware of/monitor habits you have that indicate you are over-breathing. This includes the following:
- Hearing yourself breathe while you are relaxed
- Regular sighing and sniffing
- Taking large breaths before speaking
- Frequent yawning
- Upper chest movement while breathing

2. Learn to counteract and limit these behaviors:
- If you can hear that your breathing is heavy, focus on slowing your breath and feeling the air in your nostrils. Try to quiet yourself.
- If you notice regular sighing and sniffing, stop yourself in the act. Pause your breathing for 3-5 seconds, then swallow instead.
- If you take large breaths before speaking, try to prevent the breath, and take a drink of water or swallow instead.
- If you are yawning frequently, practice stifling the yawn or do it with a closed mouth.
- If you feel a lot of upper chest movement while breathing, then try to focus on taking slow breaths into your diaphragm, and focus on shifting the movement to that area instead.

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

title: Module 5 (Post-Frenectomy)
introduction: Follow your post-operative protocol carefully to ensure proper healing and prevent reattachment.
objectives: Follow all post-operative protocol
Continue with recommended exercises to prevent frenum reattachment
Incorporate Mouth Taping to encourage nasal breathing

exercises.name: Days 1-3: Minimal Activity Protocol
exercises.description:
- Minimal Activity to avoid sutures coming out
- Try to talk and eat as normally as possible but avoid spicy, crunchy, sharp and acidic foods
- Soft, cold foods are best
- Gently lift tongue up and down, left and right
- Floor of Mouth Massage

exercises.name: Gentle Lingual Palatal Suction (Days 4-7)
exercises.description: Keep your lips back in a smile. Open mouth as wide as possible without floor of mouth lifting. Use gentle suction to hold the tongue to the roof of your mouth and hold for as long as possible (Start with 10 seconds and work up to 1 minute). Try focusing on lifting the back portion of the tongue to the palate. If tongue is overflowing you can slowly open and close teeth together to help tongue squish inside the arch. Using the provided chopsticks to push the tongue inside of the arch can also help.
compensations: Watch in mirror for facial grimace (chin strain/dimpling), neck engagement, jaw protrusion and jaw lateralization, floor of mouth activation -- use bite block to avoid

exercises.name: Gentle Tongue Trace (Days 4-7)
exercises.description: Try with your mouth open, halfway or closed. Place the tip of the tongue against the spot and trace back along the palate in a straight line and far back as possible. You should feel a stretch in the frenum and floor of your mouth.
compensations: Watch in mirror for jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion. Use bite block to avoid
Scan for head/neck/facial/body tension and try to ease

exercises.name: Floor of Mouth Massage (Days 4-7)
exercises.description: Massage muscles along the floor of the mouth to release tension. Be slow and gentle, focusing on 1 area at a time.

exercises.name: Mouth Taping (Daytime)
props: Mouth tape (Micropore or Surgical Tape recommended)
exercises.description:
1. Make sure you have tape that will stick and not irritate your skin (A starting amount is included in kit, but more should be purchased, consider using Micropore or Surgical Tape as they are breathable)
2. Use lip balm or Chapstick if your lips are dry, before applying the tape.
3. Place the tape however you are most comfortable -- vertically, horizontally, or in an X pattern. I recommend sealing off the air unless you are uncomfortable with this feeling.
4. Find a 1 hour quiet activity you can do to let the time pass (during screen time, reading, in the car, cooking etc).

- If you cannot find a full hour to use the tape, just do it for as long as you can. You can always come back to add more time later. Remember that a continuous hour is better than a split-up hour if you can do it.

Tips:
- You may not be able to do a full hour. I recommend starting with 5 or 10 minutes and adding more time depending on how well you do.
- A referral may be needed to address nasal breathing if you cannot complete this exercise
- If you feel claustrophobic while doing this exercise, practice the Belly Breathing Exercise to help relax yourself and adjust your breathing, before applying the tape
- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first
- Start applying mouth tape over night once comfortable with longer periods of time. This will be added as a second passive exercise in future modules.

exercises.name: Lingual Palatal Suction (Days 8-14)
exercises.description: See exercise notes above. Okay to use stronger suction again.

exercises.name: Tongue Trace (Days 8-14)
exercises.description: See exercise notes above.

exercises.name: Mouth Taping (Daytime)
props: Mouth tape (Micropore or Surgical Tape recommended)
exercises.description:
1. Make sure you have tape that will stick and not irritate your skin (A starting amount is included in kit, but more should be purchased, consider using Micropore or Surgical Tape as they are breathable)
2. Use lip balm or Chapstick if your lips are dry, before applying the tape.
3. Place the tape however you are most comfortable -- vertically, horizontally, or in an X pattern. I recommend sealing off the air unless you are uncomfortable with this feeling.
4. Find a 1 hour quiet activity you can do to let the time pass (during screen time, reading, in the car, cooking etc).

- If you cannot find a full hour to use the tape, just do it for as long as you can. You can always come back to add more time later. Remember that a continuous hour is better than a split-up hour if you can do it.

Tips:
- You may not be able to do a full hour. I recommend starting with 5 or 10 minutes and adding more time depending on how well you do.
- A referral may be needed to address nasal breathing if you cannot complete this exercise
- If you feel claustrophobic while doing this exercise, practice the Belly Breathing Exercise to help relax yourself and adjust your breathing, before applying the tape
- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first
- Start applying mouth tape over night once comfortable with longer periods of time. This will be added as a second passive exercise in future modules.

title: Module 7
introduction: You are now post-frenectomy. Begin rebuilding swallowing patterns and maintain your tongue stretches.
objectives: Learn the building blocks for correct swallowing technique with the perfect bowl exercise
Improve lip seal by strengthening the surrounding muscles
Continue with post operative tongue stretches
Maintain nasal breathing during light activity
Maintain proper mid-tongue to palate positioning with elastic hold exercise for an extended time
Maintain nasal breathing overnight by using mouth tape

exercises.name: Perfect Bowl
exercises.description:
Mouth should be open about half way (practice with a smile for 1 minute and relaxed face for 1 minute), the tongue should form a bowl shape by sinking into the floor of the mouth and curling the sides and tip up. The first goal is to successfully make the correct shape with the tongue. Once this is achieved, the second goal is to hold the shape for 10 seconds or longer without compensations.

- Watch in the mirror to avoid lips rounding, neck engagement, jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion. Make sure this can be done with a relaxed/still face as well as with cheeks/lips back in a smile.
- The tongue may want to rise up high in the mouth while making the bowl shape. If this happens, practice lowering the tongue into the floor of the mouth in a normal shape first. If you struggle rolling the sides, a chopstick can be used to gently press the center of the tongue down, or a band can be placed on the surface of the tongue as a target to "wrap the edges around". A small candy or goldfish cracker can be used to make this more fun for kids.
- This exercise can be difficult to master and you may not be able to accomplish it until you have a lightbulb moment and then it will be easier afterwards. Keep practicing and don't be discouraged!

exercises.name: Lip Pops
exercises.description: Hold the lips between the teeth for 5 seconds and pop them out with a clear and loud sound.
compensations: This exercise may cause facial tension/pain for people with TMD. Important to limit tension, neck engagement and jaw protrusion to prevent

exercises.name: Lingual Palatal Suction (Alternating)
exercises.description: Keep your lips back in a smile. Open mouth as wide as possible without floor of mouth lifting. Use strong suction to hold the tongue to the roof of your mouth and hold for as long as possible (Start with 10 seconds and work up to 1 minute). Try focusing on lifting the back portion of the tongue to the palate.
compensations: Watch in mirror for facial grimace (chin strain/dimpling), neck engagement, jaw protrusion and jaw lateralization, floor of mouth activation – use bite block to avoid
exercises.notes: Alternate each practice session between this exercise and the other two alternating exercises.

exercises.name: Tongue Stretch (Alternating)
exercises.description: Pull the tongue straight out of the mouth. Avoid tensing tongue, jaw, neck and face.
exercises.notes: Alternate each practice session between this exercise and the other two alternating exercises.

exercises.name: Tongue Trace (Alternating)
exercises.description: Try with your mouth open, halfway or closed. Place the tip of the tongue against the spot and trace back along the palate in a straight line and as far back as possible. You should feel a stretch in the frenum and floor of your mouth.
compensations: Watch in mirror for jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion - use bite block to avoid
Scan for head/neck/facial/body tension and try to ease
exercises.notes: Alternate each practice session between this exercise and the other two alternating exercises.

exercises.name: Take a 15-Minute Walk: Nasal Breathing Only
exercises.description:
1. Walk for 15 minutes, concentrating on breathing through your nose, ideally using mouth tape.
2. Keep a casual but steady pace. If you feel winded or lacking oxygen, just slow down. If you need to stop completely to slow your breathing, that is okay too. Just don't allow yourself to breathe through your mouth. If this is easy for you, then you can try speeding up, walking up a hill, or jogging for a short distance.

Tips:
- Avoid talking, laughing, sighing, yawning, sniffing, and any other signs of over breathing that your body may try to get you to do.
- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first

exercises.name: Middle of Tongue – One Elastic Hold
exercises.description:
Place elastic on the middle of the tongue and hold it to the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

exercises.name: Mouth Taping Overnight
props: Mouth tape (Micropore or Surgical Tape recommended)
exercises.description:
1. Make sure you have tape that will stick and not irritate your skin (A starting amount is included in kit, but more should be purchased, consider using Micropore or Surgical Tape as they are breathable)
2. Use lip balm or Chapstick if your lips are dry, before applying the tape.
3. Place the tape however you are most comfortable -- vertically, horizontally, or in an X pattern. I recommend sealing off the air unless you are uncomfortable with this feeling.

Tips:
- You may not be able to do a full night yet. Continue adding more time during the day until you do.
- A referral may be needed to address nasal breathing if you cannot complete this exercise
- If you feel claustrophobic while doing this exercise, practice the Belly Breathing Exercise to help relax yourself and adjust your breathing, before applying the tape
- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first

progress_benchmark: Benchmark check: You should expect 50% improvement or 50% total with tongue posture, nasal breathing and lip seal.

title: Module 8
introduction: This module introduces swallowing retraining and a midway breathing check-in.
objectives: Strengthen internal tongue musculature and improve tongue coordination and posture with the pickle tongue exercise
Develop the skills for a new swallowing pattern through repetition with the smile swallows exercise
Practice tongue-mandible dissociation and tongue coordination with the teeth trace exercise
Complete a midway check in for breathing health with BOLT test
Maintain proper mid-tongue to palate positioning with elastic hold exercise for an extended time
Maintain nasal breathing overnight by using mouth tape

exercises.name: Pickle Tongue
exercises.description:
Focus on narrowing the sides and rounding the top of the tongue while sticking the tongue out of the mouth. If you are unable to narrow tongue, go back to the Tongue Points exercise (from Module 2). If you are unable to activate the muscles to round the top surface of the tongue, use a toothbrush to brush the top of the tongue or slide along front teeth.

Must be able to hold shape for 10-20 seconds without compensations after practice
compensations: Watch in mirror for facial grimace (pursing of lips), neck engagement, and jaw protrusion
Can use bite block on molars to prevent jaw protrusion

exercises.name: Smile Swallows
exercises.description:
Squeeze water from pipette into mouth and with cheeks back in a big smile swallow the water

This exercise is not meant to replicate natural drinking. It is designed to help learn a new swallowing pattern through repetition and neuromuscular re-education.

If this is difficult for you practice lingual palatal suction prior
compensations: Watch in mirror for neck engagement, facial grimace (lips rounding), lips trying to close during swallow - bite on chopstick to help avoid
Watch in mirror for Tongue Thrust (Pushing tongue into the teeth to swallow, tongue should push against the palate)

exercises.name: Teeth Trace/Lip Licks
exercises.description: The mouth should be open half way. Practice with cheeks back in a smile and relaxed face. Tongue movements should be as precise and slow as possible. The smallest surface on the tip of the tongue should be used to trace the lips (or teeth if lips are too difficult). The goal is to have the tongue trace only the pink part of the lips, and not move onto the surrounding skin of the face.
compensations: Avoid neck engagement and jaw lateralization during exercise to help with tongue-jaw dissociation
Continue with nasal breathing (Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first)

exercises.name: BOLT Test (Midway Check In)
exercises.description:
This test measures breathing health and progress.

Good for adults with sleep apnea, snoring, chronic fatigue/tiredness, and brain fog.

Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first

Scale of what is considered healthy (higher score means organs/tissues are receiving adequate oxygen):
• 40-60 seconds is healthy and ideal
• 30-39 seconds indicates mild breathing/airway issues
• 20-29 seconds indicates moderate breathing/airway issues
• 10-19 seconds indicates unhealthy breathing
• 0-9 seconds is very unhealthy breathing and severe airway issues

Instructions:
1. Take a small, silent breath in and a small, silent breath out.
2. Hold your nose with your fingers to prevent air from entering your lungs while timing yourself with a stopwatch
3. At the first sign of "air hunger", you will also feel the first involuntary movements of your breathing muscles. Your stomach may jerk, or the area around your neck may contract.
4. Release your nose and stop the timer at these first signs of "air hunger". This will tell you your initial score.

Tips:
- When you release your nose, your inhalation should remain calm. If you are breathing heavily or taking deep breaths, then you waited too long and the score is not correct. Keep in mind that this is not a breath-holding test.

exercises.description:
Place elastic on the middle of the tongue and hold it to the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

props: Mouth tape (Micropore or Surgical Tape recommended)
exercises.description:
1. Make sure you have tape that will stick and not irritate your skin (A starting amount is included in kit, but more should be purchased, consider using Micropore or Surgical Tape as they are breathable)

title: Module 9
introduction: This module advances swallowing to liquids and soft foods while continuing to build breathing control.
objectives: Help control liquid and soft foods in the mouth by forming a bolus and practice proper swallowing with water and soft food trap
Encourage higher tongue posture and tongue coordination with middle tongue push
Improve lip strength and lip seal with button pulls
Work on reducing your breathing
Maintain proper mid-tongue to palate positioning with elastic hold exercise for an extended time
Maintain nasal breathing overnight by using mouth tape

exercises.name: Water Trap and Soft Food Trap
props: Soft Food (Yogurt, Pudding, or Apple Sauce)
exercises.description:
We will be advancing perfect bowl/smile swallow/lingual palatal suction exercises from previous modules for this swallowing exercise. Start with small sips/spoonfuls (advancing to larger ones once able) and trap in a perfect bowl against the roof of your mouth. Push tongue against roof of mouth into lingual palatal suction shape for swallowing. Cheeks, lips, and teeth should be in a smile during exercise.

If struggling with exercise practice Lingual Palatal Suction, Perfect Bowl or Smile Swallows from previous modules first
compensations: Watch in the mirror, focusing on eliminating facial grimace (chin dimpling/strain and lips pursing) neck engagement or head bob during swallow.
Tongue Thrust (Pushing tongue into the teeth to swallow, tongue should push against the palate)

exercises.name: Middle Tongue Push
exercises.description:
This exercise should make the tongue appear to be the shape of a ball -- the dorsal surface should appear rounded and the tongue should be pulled back in the mouth. Think of Pickle Tongue exercise without tongue protruding outside of the mouth. The tip of the tongue should not be touching the teeth, the spot, or the palate. It should be "hovering" in the middle of the mouth in an open posture.

* Start with 3 seconds and work up to 10
*Can try adding kuh sound to beginning of exercise to form shape
compensations: Avoid jaw protrusion, keep mouth halfway open, avoid pursing lips and neck engagement

exercises.name: Button Pulls
exercises.description: Place the button in front of the teeth and behind the lips. Pull hard enough with string parallel to the floor to feel lip muscle activation. 20 seconds out the front, left, and right sides. Repeat twice for a total of 2 minutes.
compensations: Watching in the mirror for jaw protrusion - bite teeth gently together to avoid.
Use muscles around lips rather than chin muscle.

exercises.name: Reduced Breathing
exercises.description:
Helps to improve BOLT score

Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first

Instructions:
1. Start this exercise by doing the BOLT Test (see previous module of exercises)
2. This time, instead of releasing your nose to take your bolt score measurement, extend your breath hold for 5-10 seconds longer. This should create a feeling of air hunger, or the desire to breathe heavily when you release your nose.
3. Release your nose and start your timer.
4. Do not allow yourself to gasp in air through your mouth or to take deep breaths. Instead, focus on breathing through your nose for the next 2 minutes at a "reduced rate".
5. This means that rather than inhaling a full breath, you need to inhale less -- about 10% less than you would normally.
6. For the 2-minute duration, you should feel like you are maintaining a slightly oxygen-deprived state. It should not feel too difficult, just a slight struggle.
7. Concentrate on breathing slowly, into your belly or diaphragm, and the feeling of the air in your nostrils. Count to 5 as you inhale, then pause for 3 with your lungs filled, and count to 5 as you exhale is a good way to stay focused on your breathing.
8. When the 2 minutes are up, release your nose, and allow your breathing to return to normal. Do not take deep or gasping breaths, just normal-sized breaths.
9. Breathe normally for 1 minute and then decrease your breathing to the "reduced rate" (10% less) and repeat for 2 more minutes, followed by 1 minute of normal breathing as a recovery.
10. Repeat this for a total of 3 cycles -- it should take about 10 minutes total including the BOLT Test at the beginning.

Tips:
- If this exercise is too difficult, you are probably reducing your breathing too much.
- Remember that you only need to do the BOLT test at the beginning of the exercise. It is meant to be done only once, not at the beginning of each cycle.

exercises.description:
Place elastic on the middle of the tongue and hold it to the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

7. Concentrate on breathing slowly, into your belly or diaphragm, and the feeling of the air in your nostrils. Count to 5 as you inhale, then pause for 3 with your lungs filled, and count to 5 as you exhale is a good way to stay focused on your breathing.
8. When the 2 minutes are up, release your nose, and allow your breathing to return to normal. Do not take deep or gasping breaths, just normal-sized breaths.
9. Breathe normally for 1 minute and then decrease your breathing to the "reduced rate" (10% less) and repeat for 2 more minutes, followed by 1 minute of normal breathing as a recovery.
10. Repeat this for a total of 3 cycles -- it should take about 10 minutes total including the BOLT Test at the beginning.

Tips:
- If this exercise is too difficult, you are probably reducing your breathing too much.
- Remember that you only need to do the BOLT test at the beginning of the exercise. It is meant to be done only once, not at the beginning of each cycle.

exercises.name: Middle of Tongue – One Elastic Hold
exercises.description:
Place elastic on the middle of the tongue and hold it to the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

exercises.name: Mouth Taping Overnight
props: Mouth tape (Micropore or Surgical Tape recommended)
exercises.description:
1. Make sure you have tape that will stick and not irritate your skin (A starting amount is included in kit, but more should be purchased, consider using Micropore or Surgical Tape as they are breathable)
2. Use lip balm or Chapstick if your lips are dry, before applying the tape.
3. Place the tape however you are most comfortable -- vertically, horizontally, or in an X pattern. I recommend sealing off the air unless you are uncomfortable with this feeling.

Tips:
- You may not be able to do a full night yet. Continue adding more time during the day until you do.
- A referral may be needed to address nasal breathing if you cannot complete this exercise
- If you feel claustrophobic while doing this exercise, practice the Belly Breathing Exercise to help relax yourself and adjust your breathing, before applying the tape
- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first

title: Module 10
introduction: This module adds a second elastic and advances swallowing to harder foods.
objectives: Continue to work on proper swallowing technique with single sips
Practice bolus formation with harder foods and get used to chewing food thoroughly using teeth only with the cracker chew and check exercise
Learn to relieve facial tension and help lip seal with horse lips exercise
Use posture exercises to help encourage deeper breathing
Maintain proper tongue to palate suction by adding a second elastic to the elastic hold exercise
Maintain nasal breathing overnight by using mouth tape

exercises.name: Single Sips
exercises.description:
We will be advancing the Water Trap exercise from the previous module's exercises for this swallowing exercise. Single Sips is more about mastery of the concepts and doing the exercise quickly with lots of repetition so that it becomes second nature.

Start with small sips/spoonfuls (advancing to larger ones once able) and trap in a perfect bowl against the roof of your mouth. Push tongue against roof of mouth into lingual palatal suction shape for swallowing. Cheeks, lips, and teeth should be in a smile during exercise.

Once the concept is mastered using a regular glass of water, practice drinking the same way, except from different containers. For example: bottle, can, thermos, straw, mug, etc. This exercise can also be done with a neutral or relaxed face once mastered with a smile
compensations: Watch in the mirror, focusing on eliminating facial grimace (chin dimpling and lips pursing) neck engagement or head bob during swallow.
Tongue Thrust (Pushing tongue into the teeth to swallow, tongue should push against the palate)

exercises.name: Cracker Chew and Check
props: Crackers (Goldfish, Ritz, Pretzels, Wheat Thins, Cheez-Its, rice crackers, or plantain chips)
exercises.description: Chew a cracker for 20-30 chews (this will feel like a lot -- important to chew more than what you are comfortable with, in order to start chewing food more thoroughly in general). Have an awareness on how you are chewing and activate chewing muscles -- avoid sucking on the cracker or mashing it with tongue which activates the lips and cheek muscles. Form a bolus (ball-like mixture of food/saliva) before swallowing -- use the same swallowing technique that has been practiced over the last few modules.
compensations: Watch in the mirror, focusing on eliminating facial grimace (chin dimpling and lips pursing) neck engagement or head bob during swallow.
Tongue Thrust (Pushing tongue into the teeth to swallow, tongue should push against the palate)

exercises.name: Horse Lips
exercises.description:
Start with lips pressed together and blow out through mouth to create a horse sound

If you struggle to achieve lip seal start with pressing/flattening lips between fingers

For patients with lots of facial tension, it may be helpful to stretch out the cheeks by placing the thumb inside the cheek, fingers on the outside of the cheek, and pushing out to create a stretch
compensations: Watch in a mirror to avoid facial grimace (rounding the lips or dimpling the chin) when pressing lips together
Continue nasal breathing throughout exercise (Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first)

exercises.name: Shoulder Rolls
exercises.description:
With the tongue on the spot and mouth closed, stand or sit with good posture

Roll your shoulders slowly from front to back. Hold them back as far as possible, hold there for the count of 3

Repeat for 1 minute, using slow rotations of the shoulders

exercises.name: Middle of Tongue – Two Elastic Hold
exercises.description:
Place elastic on the tip of the tongue and middle of the tongue and hold it to the spot and the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

exercises.name: Mouth Taping Overnight
props: Mouth tape (Micropore or Surgical Tape recommended)
exercises.description:
1. Make sure you have tape that will stick and not irritate your skin (A starting amount is included in kit, but more should be purchased, consider using Micropore or Surgical Tape as they are breathable)
2. Use lip balm or Chapstick if your lips are dry, before applying the tape.
3. Place the tape however you are most comfortable -- vertically, horizontally, or in an X pattern. I recommend sealing off the air unless you are uncomfortable with this feeling.

Tips:
- You may not be able to do a full night yet. Continue adding more time during the day until you do.
- A referral may be needed to address nasal breathing if you cannot complete this exercise
- If you feel claustrophobic while doing this exercise, practice the Belly Breathing Exercise to help relax yourself and adjust your breathing, before applying the tape
- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first

title: Module 11
introduction: This module refines swallowing with a neutral face and introduces lateral posture work.
objectives: Learn to swallow without the use of facial muscles with frozen face single sips
Learn proper swallowing technique with a broader spectrum of foods in 20 foods exercise
Improve mid-face muscle tone with cheek lifts
Use posture exercises to help encourage deeper breathing
Maintain proper tongue to palate suction with two elastic hold exercise for an extended time
Maintain nasal breathing overnight by using mouth tape

exercises.name: Single Sips - Frozen Face
exercises.description:
This exercise is taking the concept of Water Trap and doing the exercise quickly so it becomes second nature. Once the concept is mastered with a glass of water, practice from other containers (bottle, can, thermos, straw, mug etc). Start with small sips/spoonfuls (advancing to larger ones once able) and trap in a perfect bowl against the roof of your mouth. Push tongue against roof of mouth into lingual palatal suction shape for swallowing.

The face should be neutral and should not move during this exercise
compensations: Avoid facial grimace (pursing of lips, tightening cheeks, and chin dimpling), moving/bobbing head, or neck engagement
Tongue Thrust (Pushing tongue into the teeth to swallow, tongue should push against the palate)
If you struggle with this exercise, go back and practice smile swallows or water trap first

exercises.name: 20 Foods
props: 20 different foods between sessions, 5 bites of each (crunchy or harder foods ideally)
exercises.description:
This exercise is a progression of the Cracker Chew and Check, but this time different foods are used. Take a regular sized bite of the food, chew thoroughly - at least 20 times. When the food is soft enough to swallow, form a bolus (small rounded mass of chewed food/saliva), then swallow the food with the teeth together, lips/cheeks back, and tongue on the Spot. A second, "clean-up swallow" should be done to collect the remaining bits of food within the mouth.

Used for developing coordination of tongue and oral muscles to achieve functional chewing and swallowing. Gain awareness of chewing thoroughly, using the teeth to chew and not the tongue to suck on food while eating, and manipulation and control of food within the oral cavity.
compensations: Avoid facial grimace (pursing of lips, tightening cheeks, and chin dimpling), moving/bobbing head, or neck engagement
Tongue Thrust (Pushing tongue into the teeth to swallow, tongue should push against the palate)
Scan face, head, neck, and shoulders for tension or pain during exercise and try to release it

exercises.name: Cheek Lifts
exercises.description:
Smile on each side of face -- you may wink or squint one eye closed to help active midface muscles. Watch to make sure the muscles of the cheek and under eye are moving

To make exercise more difficult you can try lifting your nostril/lip on each side

Troubleshooting: If you struggle to use the midface muscles, start with tapping/brushing the pads of the fingers across the cheeks. This will create sensory input and help stimulate the nerves and muscles.
compensations: People with weak midface muscles will often smile with their lower face causing neck engagement. The smile line will often be angled downward or straight across. Work on smiling in an "upward" direction with the corner of mouth and think about cheeks when smiling

exercises.name: Standing Side Bends
exercises.description:
With the tongue on the spot and mouth closed, stand with your back against the wall, with feet shoulder width apart

Place your left palm on the left side of your head near your temple

Guide your head and tilt your body towards the right until you feel a stretch along the left side of your body, shoulder and neck

Take three to ten breaths, ensuring your mouth is closed and tongue is still on the spot

Switch sides and repeat for 2 minutes

Roll shoulder slowly from front to back. Hold them back as far as possible, hold there for the count of 3

Repeat for 1 minute, using slow rotations of the shoulders

exercises.name: Middle of Tongue – Two Elastic Hold
exercises.description:
Place elastic on the tip of the tongue and middle of the tongue and hold it to the spot and the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

exercises.name: Mouth Taping Overnight
props: Mouth tape (Micropore or Surgical Tape recommended)
exercises.description:
1. Make sure you have tape that will stick and not irritate your skin (A starting amount is included in kit, but more should be purchased, consider using Micropore or Surgical Tape as they are breathable)
2. Use lip balm or Chapstick if your lips are dry, before applying the tape.
3. Place the tape however you are most comfortable -- vertically, horizontally, or in an X pattern. I recommend sealing off the air unless you are uncomfortable with this feeling.

Tips:
- You may not be able to do a full night yet. Continue adding more time during the day until you do.
- A referral may be needed to address nasal breathing if you cannot complete this exercise
- If you feel claustrophobic while doing this exercise, practice the Belly Breathing Exercise to help relax yourself and adjust your breathing, before applying the tape
- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first

title: Module 12
introduction: This module transitions swallowing to real meals and introduces alternating posture exercises.
objectives: Practice proper swallowing technique while eating and drinking without using facial muscles
Work on improving lip seal by placing tubes under lips
Use posture exercises to help encourage deeper breathing
Maintain proper tongue to palate suction with two elastic hold exercise for an extended time
Maintain nasal breathing overnight by using mouth tape

exercises.name: Continuous Drinking
exercises.description:
Drink and swallow multiple times in a row with teeth together using swallowing technique practiced before with single sips exercise

Drinking with teeth together will allow you to drink without bringing air into the mouth and swallowing air

Swallow should be silent as a result
compensations: Tongue Thrust (Pushing tongue into the teeth to swallow, tongue should push against the palate)
Sound during swallow - hold teeth together and avoid bringing air into mouth

exercises.name: Perfect Bites - Frozen Face
exercises.description:
This exercise is a progression of the 20 Foods Exercise, but this time a more complex meal is used. Take a regular sized bite of the food, chew thoroughly - at least 20 times. When the food is soft enough to swallow, form a bolus (small rounded mass of chewed food/saliva), then swallow the food with a neutral face. A second, "clean-up swallow" should be done to collect the remaining bits of food within the mouth.

Used for developing coordination of tongue and oral muscles to achieve normal eating, chewing and swallowing. This exercise helps with awareness of chewing thoroughly, using the teeth to chew and not the tongue to suck on food while eating, and manipulation and control of food within the oral cavity. This is the last chewing/swallowing exercise as you transition from eating food as an exercise into eating food this way more naturally.
compensations: Tongue Thrust (Pushing tongue into the teeth to swallow, tongue should push against the palate)
Avoid facial grimace (movement of lips, tightening cheeks, and chin dimpling), moving/bobbing head, or neck engagement
Scan face, head, neck, and shoulders for tension or pain during exercise and try to release it

exercises.name: Tubes Under Lips
exercises.description: Tubes are placed under the lips and the lips must create a seal around the tubes. Once the tubes are removed it should feel very easy to make a lip seal
compensations: Watch in the mirror for facial grimace (pursed lips, strained/dimpled chin). Touch/rub the chin to help it relax and focus on using the lip muscles instead of the chin to hold the tubes in place

exercises.name: Goal Post Arms
exercises.frequency: 1x/day ALTERNATING with Head and Neck Tilts
exercises.description:
With the tongue on the spot and mouth closed, stand with good posture with your back against a wall

Hold your arms straight out to the sides at right angles (like a goal post) with your palms facing forward. Your shoulders and elbows should be level

Slide your arms up and down along the wall (only about 3-5 inches), concentrating on proper breathing technique, and keeping your head, body and arms all aligned

This exercise helps stretch out tight chest muscles

exercises.name: Head and Neck Tilts
exercises.description:
Start with arms out in Goal Post position

Keeping your arms aligned with the body, move your hands behind your head so that only your middle fingers touch.

Tilt your head forward so your chin tucks into your neck. Hold for 5 seconds

Slowly tilt your head back until it drops as far back as it can go. Your fingers may support your neck but should not restrict the range of motion of your head. Hold for 5 seconds

Repeat for 2 minutes while holding your tongue to the spot, keeping your mouth closed and nasal breathing

exercises.name: Middle of Tongue – Two Elastic Hold
exercises.description:
Place elastic on the tip of the tongue and middle of the tongue and hold it to the spot and the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

exercises.name: Mouth Taping Overnight
props: Mouth tape (Micropore or Surgical Tape recommended)
exercises.description:
1. Make sure you have tape that will stick and not irritate your skin (A starting amount is included in kit, but more should be purchased, consider using Micropore or Surgical Tape as they are breathable)
2. Use lip balm or Chapstick if your lips are dry, before applying the tape.
3. Place the tape however you are most comfortable -- vertically, horizontally, or in an X pattern. I recommend sealing off the air unless you are uncomfortable with this feeling.

Tips:
- You may not be able to do a full night yet. Continue adding more time during the day until you do.
- A referral may be needed to address nasal breathing if you cannot complete this exercise
- If you feel claustrophobic while doing this exercise, practice the Belly Breathing Exercise to help relax yourself and adjust your breathing, before applying the tape
- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first

exercises.name: Self Study (Active Exercises)
exercises.description:
Look back through the active exercises in this program and review 3 of the exercises that you struggled with the most previously

Practice these exercises for the next 2 modules

exercises.name: Head Pushes
exercises.description:
With the tongue on the spot and mouth closed, stand with your back against the wall, with feet shoulder width apart and good posture

Look straight forward, and without tipping your head or changing your eye level, push the back of your head straight back towards the wall. Hold for 5 seconds

Bring head back to resting position for 3 seconds

Repeat for one minute

*Make sure your hair is not in a ponytail, bun or clip - this will further the distance of your head from the wall

exercises.name: Shoulder Squeezes
exercises.description:
With the tongue on the spot and mouth closed, stand with your back against the wall, with feet shoulder width apart and good posture

Place a hand towel folded and rolled up into a tube shape between your shoulder blades

Squeeze your shoulder blades together to squeeze the towel while moving your shoulders and elbows slightly back behind you. Hold for 5 seconds.

Go back to resting position for 3 seconds

Repeat for one minute

*Make sure your hair is not in a ponytail, bun or clip - this will further the distance of your head from the wall

exercises.name: BOLT Test (Final Check In)
exercises.description:
This test measures breathing health and progress.

Good for adults with sleep apnea, snoring, chronic fatigue/tiredness, and brain fog.

Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first

Scale of what is considered healthy (higher score means organs/tissues are receiving adequate oxygen):
• 40-60 seconds is healthy and ideal
• 30-39 seconds indicates mild breathing/airway issues
• 20-29 seconds indicates moderate breathing/airway issues
• 10-19 seconds indicates unhealthy breathing
• 0-9 seconds is very unhealthy breathing and severe airway issues

Instructions:
1. Take a small, silent breath in and a small, silent breath out.
2. Hold your nose with your fingers to prevent air from entering your lungs while timing yourself with a stopwatch
3. At the first sign of "air hunger", you will also feel the first involuntary movements of your breathing muscles. Your stomach may jerk, or the area around your neck may contract.
4. Release your nose and stop the timer at these first signs of "air hunger". This will tell you your initial score.

Tips:
- When you release your nose, your inhalation should remain calm. If you are breathing heavily or taking deep breaths, then you waited too long and the score is not correct. Keep in mind that this is not a breath-holding test.

exercises.name: Middle of Tongue – Two Elastic Hold
exercises.description:
Place elastic on the tip of the tongue and middle of the tongue and hold it to the spot and the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

exercises.name: Mouth Taping Overnight
props: Mouth tape (Micropore or Surgical Tape recommended)
exercises.description:
1. Make sure you have tape that will stick and not irritate your skin (A starting amount is included in kit, but more should be purchased, consider using Micropore or Surgical Tape as they are breathable)
2. Use lip balm or Chapstick if your lips are dry, before applying the tape.
3. Place the tape however you are most comfortable -- vertically, horizontally, or in an X pattern. I recommend sealing off the air unless you are uncomfortable with this feeling.

Tips:
- You may not be able to do a full night yet. Continue adding more time during the day until you do.
- A referral may be needed to address nasal breathing if you cannot complete this exercise
- If you feel claustrophobic while doing this exercise, practice the Belly Breathing Exercise to help relax yourself and adjust your breathing, before applying the tape
- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first

title: Module 14 – Post-Program Review and Maintenance
introduction: Congratulations on finishing your Myofunctional Therapy Program!
post_program_text:
Congratulations on finishing your 24 week Myofunctional Therapy Program! By completing this program you should notice improvements in your mouth breathing, tongue posture, lip seal, and swallowing. You may also experience less facial/TMD pain and headaches, less clenching and grinding, improved sleep quality, improved sleep apnea scores, better digestion and reduced allergy symptoms. Look back and review how far you have come by reviewing your first and last BOLT scores and weekly progress charts.

Moving forward you should no longer need to continue practicing exercises as long as you continue to accomplish each of the four goals. You will want to ensure you are nasal breathing and have the correct tongue posture 95+% of the time. While eating make sure you continue to chew with your mouth closed, avoid overly large bites, and chew thoroughly using the teeth while avoiding mashing or sucking on food with the tongue. It is also important that the tongue is on the spot when swallowing and not pushing forward into the teeth (tongue thrust). Facial grimace should be avoided and muscles should always be relaxed while swallowing. The same rules apply when drinking with the addition of ensuring you are swallowing quietly.

title: Module 1
introduction: This module is about lifting your tongue smoothly and evenly.
objectives: Perform click and tick-tock exercises to strengthen tongue and mid face muscles
Complete tongue brushing for neuromuscular re-education
Practice tongue trace movements to stretch the frenum
Establish baseline breathing health with BOLT test
Maintain tongue-to-spot positioning with elastic hold exercise

exercises.name: Clicks and Tick-Tocks
exercises.description:
Clicks (1 minute): Make repeated clicking sounds with tongue while keeping cheeks back. Have your tongue suctioned to the roof of your mouth for 3 seconds before clicking. Keep your lips back in a smile.

Tick-Tocks (1 minute): Make clicking sounds as fast as you can with good technique. This time moving lips from an exaggerated smile and do one click sound to an exaggerated pursed lip and do one click.
compensations: Watch in the mirror to prevent neck engagement, jaw protrusion and jaw lateralization. Use bite block to avoid

exercises.name: Brushing
exercises.description: Stick out the tongue and brush the full tongue 5 times. Brush the spot area 5 times. Place two surfaces together. Repeat for 1 minute. This exercise promotes neuromuscular re-education through sensory input
compensations: Watch in mirror to avoid facial grimace (rounding the lips or dimpling the chin) when protruding tongue
Continue nasal breathing throughout exercise (Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first)

exercises.name: Tongue Trace
exercises.description: Try with your mouth open, halfway or closed. Place the tip of your tongue against the spot and trace back along the palate in a straight line and far back as possible. You should feel a stretch in the frenum and floor of your mouth.
compensations: Watch in the mirror for jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion. Use bite block to avoid
Scan for head/neck/facial/body tension and try to ease

exercises.name: BOLT Test
exercises.description:
To be done on day 1, only needs to be done once.

This test measures breathing health and progress. Use the method of breathing you would have used prior to learning about myofunctional therapy.

Scale of what is considered healthy (higher score means organs/tissues are receiving adequate oxygen):
• 40-60 seconds is healthy and ideal
• 30-39 seconds indicates mild breathing/airway issues
• 20-29 seconds indicates moderate breathing/airway issues
• 10-19 seconds indicates unhealthy breathing
• 0-9 seconds is very unhealthy breathing and severe airway issues

Instructions:
1. Take a small, silent breath in and a small, silent breath out.
2. Hold your nose with your fingers to prevent air from entering your lungs while timing yourself
3. At the first sign of "air hunger", you will also feel the first involuntary movements of your breathing muscles. Your stomach may jerk, or the area around your neck may contract.
4. Release your nose and stop the timer at these first signs of "air hunger". This will tell you your initial score.

Tips:
- When you release your nose, your inhalation should remain calm. If you are breathing heavily or taking deep breaths, then you waited too long and the score is not correct. Keep in mind that this is not a breath-holding test.

exercises.name: Tongue Tip – One Elastic Hold
exercises.description:
Place elastic on the tip of the tongue and hold it to the spot

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

title: Module 2
introduction: This module, we retrain your swallow.
objectives: Strengthen posterior tongue muscles by practicing lingual palatal suction
Improve tongue coordination and tongue-mandible dissociation through tongue point exercise
Develop facial muscle tone and improve lip seal with cheek puffs
Practice diaphragmatic breathing
Maintain proper mid-tongue to palate positioning with elastic hold exercise

exercises.name: Lingual Palatal Suction
exercises.description: Keep your lips back in a smile. Open mouth as wide as possible without floor of mouth lifting. Use strong suction to hold the tongue to the roof of mouth and hold for as long as possible (Start with 10 seconds and work up to 1 minute). Try focusing on lifting the back portion of the tongue to the palate. If tongue is overflowing you can slowly open and close teeth together to help tongue squish inside the arch. Use provided chopsticks to push tongue inside of arch can also help.
compensations: Watch in mirror for facial grimace (chin strain/dimpling), neck engagement, jaw protrusion and jaw lateralization, floor of mouth activation -- use bite block to avoid
Watch for tongue overflow over teeth.

exercises.name: Tongue Points (Left and Right)
exercises.description: With a neutral face open half way. Using the smallest point of the tip of the tongue, touch the smallest surface at the corner of the mouth. Repeat for 2 minutes. Keep your lips back in a smile and open half way. Do the same tongue movements for another minute. Tongue movements should be slow and controlled without dragging the tongue along the lips.
compensations: Watch in mirror for jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion -- use bite block to avoid
Continue with nasal breathing (Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first)

exercises.name: Cheek Puffs
exercises.description: Fill your mouth with air. Move air into the right cheek and hold for 5 seconds. Move air into the left cheek and hold for 5 seconds. Move air into the top lip and hold for 5 seconds. Move air into the lower lip and hold for 5 seconds. Repeat for 2 minutes
compensations: Watch in the mirror for jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion. Keep teeth gently together to help avoid.
Continue with nasal breathing (Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first)

exercises.name: Belly/Diaphragm Breathing
exercises.description:
1. Lie on the floor face up with your knees slightly bent.
2. Place one hand lightly on your stomach and the other hand on your chest.
3. As you breathe, feel which hand is moving more, then begin to concentrate on breathing using your diaphragm (your belly), and not using your chest. Feel your stomach rise as your lungs fill with air from the bottom.
4. Let your stomach fall naturally when breathing out by relaxing your diaphragm.
5. Try placing a small object on your stomach, such as a small book or your cell phone, so that you can see the movement more easily and keep your breathing focused on that area.
6. Next, try this exercise standing or sitting up. Place your hands in the same position and focus your breathing the same way so that your stomach moves rather than your chest. This may take a bit more concentration.
7. Practice for 5 minutes at a time. Ideally, it is good to do this exercise first thing in the morning or at night before falling asleep because you are already in bed.

- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first

exercises.name: Middle of Tongue – One Elastic Hold
exercises.description:
Place elastic on the middle of the tongue and hold it to the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

title: Module 3
introduction: This module builds lateral tongue strength and establishes your breathing rhythm.
objectives: Strengthen lateral tongue muscles, improve coordination of the tongue and encourage tongue-mandible dissociation through tongue in cheek exercise
Improve posterior tongue elevation with K sounds
Improve tongue coordination, lip seal strength and tongue-mandible dissociation with tongue trace
Establish the 4-7-8 breathing pattern
Maintain proper mid-tongue to palate positioning with elastic hold exercise for an extended time

exercises.name: Tongue in Cheek
exercises.description: Push the tongue into the cheek without the jaw shifting and hold for 5 seconds. Practice with the mouth open and with lips closed together. Use slow and controlled movements. Do exercise with a still/relaxed face.
compensations: Watch in the mirror for jaw lateralization -- use bite block to avoid.
When the mouth is open, try to control for facial grimace (rounding of lips).
Check and prevent muscle bracing.

exercises.name: K Sounds (Kuh, Guh, Unk)
exercises.description: Make a clear, precise "kuh" sound with the mouth open (use a bite block and move to a taller position as you improve with exercise). The back of the tongue needs to raise up to make contact with the soft palate. The "kuh" sound helps facilitate this movement. Start with "kuh" and "guh" sounds the first week and switch to "guh" and "unk" sounds the second week of practice.
compensations: Watch in the mirror for jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion -- use bite block to avoid.
Control facial grimace (lips rounding)
Control neck engagement

exercises.name: Lip Trace
exercises.description:
While maintaining contact with the tongue under the lips, trace circles around the inside of the upper and lower lips. Alternate three times each direction. Keep your lips closed during this exercise.

Tracing with larger circles extending into the cheeks can add an extra stretch/challenge. Do smaller circles for patients who have more tongue restriction.
compensations: Watch in mirror to ensure as little jaw movement as possible - you may hold jaw or chin to minimize at first but try to limit over time
Minimize facial tension

exercises.name: 4-7-8 Breathing Pattern
exercises.description:
Instructions:
1. Set a timer for 5 minutes and begin.
2. Inhale counting to 4.
3. Before exhaling, hold your breath for a count of 7.
4. Exhale counting to 8.
5. Repeat for 5 minutes.

Tips:
- Do this anytime of day to help you relax or do it lying in bed to help you fall asleep.
- Remember to breathe only through your nose.
- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first
- Focus on inhaling the air into your belly/diaphragm.
- When you exhale, you may feel like you have to push out all your air. This is normal and helps promote deep breathing.

exercises.description:
Place elastic on the middle of the tongue and hold it to the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

title: Module 4
introduction: This module introduces swallowing foundations and breathing awareness.
objectives: Learn the building blocks for correct swallowing technique with the perfect bowl exercise
Improve lip seal by strengthening the surrounding muscles
Practice tongue-mandible dissociation and tongue coordination with the teeth trace exercise
Recognize and reduce over-breathing habits
Maintain proper mid-tongue to palate positioning with elastic hold exercise for an extended time

exercises.name: Perfect Bowl
exercises.description:
Mouth should be open about half way (practice with a smile for 1 minute and relaxed face for 1 minute), the tongue should form a bowl shape by sinking into the floor of the mouth and curling the sides and tip up. The first goal is to successfully make the correct shape with the tongue. Once this is achieved, the second goal is to hold the shape for 10 seconds or longer without compensations.

- Watch in the mirror to avoid lips rounding, neck engagement, jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion. Make sure this can be done with a relaxed/still face as well as with cheeks/lips back in a smile.
- The tongue may want to rise up high in the mouth while making the bowl shape. If this happens, practice lowering the tongue into the floor of the mouth in a normal shape first. If you struggle rolling the sides, a chopstick can be used to gently press the center of the tongue down, or a band can be placed on the surface of the tongue as a target to "wrap the edges around". A small candy or goldfish cracker can be used to make this more fun for kids.
- This exercise can be difficult to master and you may not be able to accomplish it until you have a lightbulb moment and then it will be easier afterwards. Keep practicing and don't be discouraged!

exercises.name: Lip Pops
exercises.description: Hold the lips between the teeth for 5 seconds and pop them out with a clear and loud sound.
compensations: This exercise may cause facial tension/pain for people with TMD. Important to limit tension, neck engagement and jaw protrusion to prevent

exercises.name: Teeth Trace/Upper Lip Licks
exercises.description: The mouth should be open half way. Practice with cheeks back in a smile and relaxed face. Tongue movements should be as precise and slow as possible. The smallest surface on the tip of the tongue should be used to trace the lips (or teeth if lips are too difficult). The goal is to have the tongue trace only the pink part of the lips, and not move onto the surrounding skin of the face.
compensations: Avoid neck engagement and jaw lateralization during exercise to help with tongue-jaw dissociation
Continue with nasal breathing (Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first)

exercises.name: Over Breathing Awareness
exercises.description:
When you stop mouth breathing and learn to bring your breathing volume toward normal, you have better oxygenation of your tissues and organs, including your brain.

Typical characteristics of over-breathing include mouth breathing, upper chest breathing, sighing, noticeable breathing during rest, and taking large breaths before talking

Instructions:
1. Start to become aware of/monitor habits you have that indicate you are over-breathing. This includes the following:
- Hearing yourself breathe while you are relaxed
- Regular sighing and sniffing
- Taking large breaths before speaking
- Frequent yawning
- Upper chest movement while breathing

2. Learn to counteract and limit these behaviors:
- If you can hear that your breathing is heavy, focus on slowing your breath and feeling the air in your nostrils. Try to quiet yourself.
- If you notice regular sighing and sniffing, stop yourself in the act. Pause your breathing for 3-5 seconds, then swallow instead.
- If you take large breaths before speaking, try to prevent the breath, and take a drink of water or swallow instead.
- If you are yawning frequently, practice stifling the yawn or do it with a closed mouth.
- If you feel a lot of upper chest movement while breathing, then try to focus on taking slow breaths into your diaphragm, and focus on shifting the movement to that area instead.

exercises.name: Middle of Tongue – One Elastic Hold
exercises.description:
Place elastic on the middle of the tongue and hold it to the roof of the mouth

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips

title: Module 4
introduction: This module introduces swallowing foundations and breathing awareness.
objectives: Learn the building blocks for correct swallowing technique with the perfect bowl exercise
Improve lip seal by strengthening the surrounding muscles
Practice tongue-mandible dissociation and tongue coordination with the teeth trace exercise
Recognize and reduce over-breathing habits
Maintain proper mid-tongue to palate positioning with elastic hold exercise for an extended time

exercises.name: Perfect Bowl
exercises.description:
Mouth should be open about half way (practice with a smile for 1 minute and relaxed face for 1 minute), the tongue should form a bowl shape by sinking into the floor of the mouth and curling the sides and tip up. The first goal is to successfully make the correct shape with the tongue. Once this is achieved, the second goal is to hold the shape for 10 seconds or longer without compensations.

- Watch in the mirror to avoid lips rounding, neck engagement, jaw lateralization and jaw protrusion. Make sure this can be done with a relaxed/still face as well as with cheeks/lips back in a smile.
- The tongue may want to rise up high in the mouth while making the bowl shape. If this happens, practice lowering the tongue into the floor of the mouth in a normal shape first. If you struggle rolling the sides, a chopstick can be used to gently press the center of the tongue down, or a band can be placed on the surface of the tongue as a target to "wrap the edges around". A small candy or goldfish cracker can be used to make this more fun for kids.
- This exercise can be difficult to master and you may not be able to accomplish it until you have a lightbulb moment and then it will be easier afterwards. Keep practicing and don't be discouraged!

exercises.description: Hold the lips between the teeth for 5 seconds and pop them out with a clear and loud sound.

title: Module 5
introduction: This module builds internal tongue strength and introduces daytime mouth taping.
objectives: Strengthen internal tongue musculature and improve tongue coordination and posture with the pickle tongue exercise
Develop the skills for a new swallowing pattern through repetition with the smile swallows exercise
Improve lip strength and lip seal with button pulls
Incorporate Mouth Taping to encourage nasal breathing
Maintain proper mid-tongue to palate positioning with elastic hold exercise

exercises.name: Pickle Tongue
exercises.description:
Focus on narrowing the sides and rounding the top of the tongue while sticking the tongue out of the mouth. If you are unable to narrow tongue, go back to the Tongue Points exercise (from Module 2). If you are unable to activate the muscles to round the top surface of the tongue, use a toothbrush to brush the top of the tongue or slide along front teeth.

Must be able to hold shape for 10-20 seconds without compensations after practice
compensations: Watch in mirror for facial grimace (pursing of lips), neck engagement, and jaw protrusion
Can use bite block on molars to prevent jaw protrusion

exercises.name: Smile Swallows
exercises.description:
Squeeze water from pipette into mouth and with cheeks back in a big smile swallow the water

This exercise is not meant to replicate natural drinking. It is designed to help learn a new swallowing pattern through repetition and neuromuscular re-education.

If this is difficult for you practice lingual palatal suction prior
compensations: Watch in mirror for neck engagement, facial grimace (lips rounding), lips trying to close during swallow - bite on chopstick to help avoid
Watch in mirror for Tongue Thrust (Pushing tongue into the teeth to swallow, tongue should push against the palate)

exercises.name: Button Pulls
exercises.description: Place the button in front of the teeth and behind the lips. Pull hard enough with string parallel to the floor to feel lip muscle activation. 20 seconds out the front, left, and right sides. Repeat twice for a total of 2 minutes.
compensations: Watching in the mirror for jaw protrusion - bite teeth gently together to avoid.
Use muscles around lips rather than chin muscle.

exercises.name: Mouth Taping (Daytime)
props: Mouth tape (Micropore or Surgical Tape recommended)
exercises.description:
1. Make sure you have tape that will stick and not irritate your skin (A starting amount is included in kit, but more should be purchased, consider using Micropore or Surgical Tape as they are breathable)
2. Use lip balm or Chapstick if your lips are dry, before applying the tape.
3. Place the tape however you are most comfortable -- vertically, horizontally, or in an X pattern. I recommend sealing off the air unless you are uncomfortable with this feeling.
4. Find a 1 hour quiet activity you can do to let the time pass (during screen time, reading, in the car, cooking etc).

- If you cannot find a full hour to use the tape, just do it for as long as you can. You can always come back to add more time later. Remember that a continuous hour is better than a split-up hour if you can do it.

Tips:
- You may not be able to do a full hour. I recommend starting with 5 or 10 minutes and adding more time depending on how well you do.
- A referral may be needed to address nasal breathing if you cannot complete this exercise
- If you feel claustrophobic while doing this exercise, practice the Belly Breathing Exercise to help relax yourself and adjust your breathing, before applying the tape
- Consider doing the unblocking your nose exercises first
- Start applying mouth tape over night once comfortable with longer periods of time. This will be added as a second passive exercise in future modules.

exercises.description:

If concerned about swallowing tie a piece of floss to elastic to keep between lips
